title: How AI cleaned up 15,000 recipes without breaking the site
description: A plain-English tour of the workflow behind CookJunkie's recipe audit — how the AI actually worked, and why none of it touched production data directly.
bodyMd:
## The problem

CookJunkie has 15,000 recipes. Most of them migrated cleanly from the old WordPress site, but a stubborn slice came over messy — instructions jammed into one long paragraph, difficulty levels spelled six different ways, prep and cook times missing, ingredients stuck in a freeform text blob, nutrition facts that no longer matched the recipe.

Fixing that by hand was a non-starter. Handing the whole pile to an AI in one shot was also a non-starter — no way to review, no way to roll back, and any bad output would land directly in a live cookbook.

So I built a workflow instead.

## Step 1: Pull the data down

First, copy all 15,000 recipes out of the live database into plain files — one file per recipe. The AI never points at production. It's working from a copy, and the worst thing it can do is write a wrong file.

## Step 2: Split it into manageable batches

15,000 recipes is a lot to throw at anything at once. The copies get split into small batches — just a handful of recipes each. Small enough for an AI worker to handle carefully, big enough that we're not spinning up workers for nothing.

## Step 3: Let AI work one batch at a time

For each batch, I launch ten AI workers in parallel — each handed a single recipe and a specific checklist:

- **Instructions packed into one blob.** `"a. Preheat oven b. Mix flour…"` becomes a proper step-by-step list.
- **Inconsistent difficulty.** Five variants of *easy* collapse into one.
- **Missing times.** Recipes with no prep or cook time get reasonable values.
- **Messy ingredients.** Freeform text like *3 tbsp diced tomato* gets split into quantity, unit, and base ingredient — so the site can match it to what a user actually has in their pantry.
- **Stale nutrition.** If the ingredients have changed since the last calculation, the calories and macros get recalculated to match.
- **Missing tags.** Obvious cuisines or diets (Italian, vegetarian, and so on) get filled in.

Each worker writes its proposed fixes to its own file — just the fields it wants to change. Nothing lands in the database. The output is a pile of *"here's what I'd change"* files.

## Step 4: Review before anything goes live

Because every proposal is a plain file on disk, the whole batch is reviewable as a simple before/after. I can look at exactly what the AI suggested for every recipe — accept it, edit it, or throw it out.

## Step 5: Apply, with a dry run

A separate script reads all the proposal files and pushes the changes into the database. It has a preview mode — *show me what would change, but don't write anything* — so I can sanity-check one more time before anything lands. If something goes wrong after the fact, the proposal files are still right there; revert, tweak, re-run.

## Why the separation matters

If the AI had been writing straight to the database, a single bad run could have corrupted thousands of recipes with no easy way back. Splitting the work into *propose → review → apply* keeps every step recoverable and every change auditable. The AI gets to be what it's actually good at: a fast, tireless proposer of fixes, without ever being trusted to commit them on its own.

The interesting work wasn't the prompt. It was everything around the prompt.
